ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried squid cake / chả mực; closest defensible anchor is 'squid food product' (FOODON_03315595). Exact match unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_description: Squid and cuttlefish — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for squid-based processed product; no specific code for fried squid cake exists in FoodEx2 r3.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for fried squid cake (chả mực chiên). A0CKP (Squid and cuttlefish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried squid cake or chả mực. SNOMED CT does not cover this prepared dish at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Fried Halong Squid Cake is approximately 150 g (3–4 pieces), as commonly portioned in Vietnamese restaurants and food delivery contexts.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, cooked, fried (FDC ID 175175) combined with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for regional seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC entry for fried squid (FDC ID 175175) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for sodium, fish sauce seasoning contribution, and micronutrients cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Vietnamese fish-paste preparations. The input record's per_100g values (energy 253 kcal, protein 16.7 g, fat 18.7 g, carbohydrate 5.3 g) are broadly consistent with USDA fried squid data and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 566.7 mg/100g reflects fish sauce and fish paste seasoning typical of chả mực and is consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for seasoned fried seafood preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Seafood and Protein-Rich Foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried fish cake and squid-based preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fried Halong Squid Cake (chả mực chiên). GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried fish cake and similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood preparations. The low carbohydrate content (5.3 g/100g) and high protein content (16.7 g/100g) are consistent with a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 5.3 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.2 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 566.7 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and fish paste seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 80 g per occasion (delivering ~453 mg sodium) and request preparation with reduced-sodium fish sauce or soy sauce substitution to lower the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.3 g/100g) and low GI (~40) are favourable, but high fat (18.7 g/100g) from deep-frying and high sodium (566.7 mg/100g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a small portion (80–100 g) of Fried Halong Squid Cake occasionally, as the low carbohydrate and low GI support glycaemic stability, but should monitor total fat intake from deep-frying and request reduced-sodium preparation to avoid fluid retention that can complicate diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.7 g/100g) from deep-frying, sodium 566.7 mg/100g, cholesterol 120 mg/100g, and trans fat 0.1 g/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Fried Halong Squid Cake due to its high fat, sodium, cholesterol, and trans fat content from deep-frying; if consumed, limit to a single small portion (≤80 g) and prefer grilled or steamed squid cake preparations to reduce atherogenic fat and sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium 566.7 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried squid cake due to AGE generation and high sodium; request steamed or grilled squid cake to retain the anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit of squid while eliminating the pro-inflammatory deep-frying effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Halong Squid Cake (Vietnamese: Chả Mực Chiên Hạ Long) is a celebrated Vietnamese seafood dish originating from the Halong Bay region of northern Vietnam. It is made by mincing fresh squid into a smooth paste, seasoning it with fish sauce, garlic, shallots, and black pepper, shaping the mixture into round or oval patties, and deep-frying them in hot oil until golden brown and crispy on the outside while remaining tender and chewy inside. The dish is prized for its intense umami flavour, firm yet springy texture, and the characteristic aroma of deep-fried seafood. It is commonly served as an appetiser, street food snack, or side dish, accompanied by a sweet chilli dipping sauce or Vietnamese noodle dishes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 253 kcal, 16.7 g protein, 18.7 g fat (predominantly from frying oil), and only 5.3 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with a low glycaemic index (~40). The sodium content is high at 566.7 mg/100g due to fish sauce and fish paste, which is a clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The cholesterol content of 120 mg/100g and total fat from deep-frying also elevate cardiovascular risk. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile are favourable, but portion control and sodium awareness are essential. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is considered halal.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are broadly consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fried squid (FDC ID 175175) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for seasoned fried seafood preparations. The input record's provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source. Values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained where consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese fried squid cake preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175175) or FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table at the level of specificity required. 2. trans_fat_g reported as 0.1 g/100g, consistent with minor trans fat formation during deep-frying in vegetable oil; this is not a confirmed zero. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried fish cake and high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood preparations; no direct GI measurement for chả mực chiên Hạ Long has been published. 4. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which appears to be a data entry error; this dish originates from the Halong Bay region of Vietnam and has been corrected accordingly. 5. Ethnic tags 'Pho', 'Banh Mi', 'Noodle' from the input record are not applicable to this dish and have been removed. 6. The original data_source was GrabFood (a forbidden primary source); nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FDC and FAO/INFOODS SEA approved sources before retention.
